description_long: This is an irregularly shaped stone fragment with a roughly triangular profile and a mix of dark gray and brown coloration. The stone's surface is predominantly matte and rough, with visible natural veining and some angular edges, suggesting it was broken from a larger piece. One face features a hand-written marking in black ink, reading 'BBS-15-05', which stands out against the stone's darker surface. The edges are jagged and uneven, with some areas showing a more granular, weathered texture. The stone appears dense and solid, with no visible cavities or artificial modifications beyond the marking. Its size and form suggest it could serve as a small urban obstacle or boundary marker in a park or sidewalk setting.
description: An irregular, dark gray and brown stone fragment with rough, veined surfaces and a hand-written black marking on one face. The stone is dense, jagged, and appears naturally weathered.
description_view_0: The main face is visible, showing a dark gray surface with white veining and a prominent black marking ('BBS-15-05').
description_view_1: A thin, edge-on view reveals the stone's brown, granular side and highlights its irregular, wedge-like thickness.
description_view_2: The opposite broad face is shown, mostly dark gray with subtle veining and no visible markings.
description_view_3: A side profile displays the stone's jagged, uneven edge and the transition between brown and gray surfaces.
attribute_placement: set as a standalone feature or boundary marker on the ground
attribute_pattern: Natural veining and mottling with irregular, angular edges; hand-written marking on one face.
attribute_weathering: Rough and slightly weathered, with granular edges and some surface discoloration indicating moderate exposure.
attribute_integration_with_surroundings: Contrasts with paved or grassy urban surfaces, may partially blend with other natural stones or landscaping elements.

license_info.creator_display_name: UQ School of Earth and Environmental Sciences
